activities_projects.title: MLA Word-Search
activities_projects.objective: Understand the basic terminology of state governance
steps: Find out the full form of 'MLA' (Member of Legislative Assembly)
Identify who the 'Governor' and 'Chief Minister' are in the state government
Create a simple crossword with terms like: Constituency, Majority, and Opposition
Match the terms with their easy meanings provided in the book
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Familiarizes the student with the key players in a state government
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of roles and full forms (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Mapping My Constituency
activities_projects.objective: Understand how a state is divided for elections
steps: Look at a map of your state divided into small areas (Constituencies)
Find and color the area where you live
Find out the name of the MLA currently representing your area
Identify which political party they belong to
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns that an MLA is elected from a specific area called a constituency
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of the local constituency and MLA (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Majority Rule Model
activities_projects.objective: Explain how a government is formed
steps: Imagine a state has 10 seats in total
Use different colors for different parties (e.g., Party A gets 6, Party B gets 4)
Explain that Party A has the 'Majority' because they have more than half
Identify Party B as the 'Opposition'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understand the concept of majority and opposition in the assembly
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to identify the ruling party based on numbers (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: MLA vs. Minister Roleplay
activities_projects.objective: Distinguish between a representative and a government official
steps: One student acts as an MLA (talking about people's problems)
Another acts as a Minister (talking about government action/departments)
Explain that all Ministers are MLAs, but all MLAs are not Ministers
List one department a Minister might lead (e.g., Health, Education)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that the Chief Minister selects MLAs to become Ministers
activities_projects.evaluation: Understanding the dual role of a Minister (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: A Day in the Legislative Assembly
activities_projects.objective: Learn how laws and decisions are debated
materials: Classroom chairs arranged in a semi-circle
steps: Act out a scene from the book where MLAs discuss a water shortage
Practice asking a question to a Minister about a dirty hospital
Let the 'Minister' answer by explaining what the government is doing
Identify the 'Speaker' who maintains order in the house
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes the assembly as a place for debate and accountability
activities_projects.evaluation: Participation in the debate and understanding of the Speaker's role (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Patalpuram Problem File
activities_projects.objective: Trace the steps of government action on a public issue
steps: Recall the story of Patalpuram from the chapter
List three problems the people faced (e.g., diarrhea, lack of water, dirty hospital)
Write down what the government did (e.g., press conference, water tankers)
Discuss if the Chief Minister visited the families
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Follows the chain of events from a public problem to a government response
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct listing of problems and actions from the text (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Press Conference Poster
activities_projects.objective: Learn how the government talks to the public
steps: Draw a scene of a Minister sitting with journalists and microphones
Write a headline about a government decision (e.g., 'New Toilets for Every Village')
Explain why the government holds a press conference
Mention that journalists write about this in newspapers
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the role of media in connecting the government to the people
activities_projects.evaluation: Creative representation and understanding of the press conference (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Government Departments Hunt
activities_projects.objective: Identify different branches of the state government
steps: Look at the 'Wall Newspaper' example in the book
Identify three departments mentioned (e.g., Public Works, Agriculture, Health)
Write what each department might do in one simple sentence
Discuss who is the head of all these departments (Chief Minister)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns that the government works through various specialized departments
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy in naming departments and their basic functions (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Governor's Rolecard
activities_projects.objective: Understand the ceremonial head of the state
steps: Write 'Head of the State' on a card
Write that the Governor is appointed by the Central Government
Note their job: To ensure the state government works according to the Constitution
Identify the current Governor of your state
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between the Governor (Head of State) and CM (Head of Government)
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct understanding of the Governor's appointment and role (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The 'People's Power' Diagram
activities_projects.objective: Show that the government is responsible to the people
steps: Draw a circle for 'People', an arrow to 'MLA', and an arrow to 'Government'
Label it: 'We elect them' and 'They work for us'
Add a bubble for 'Protests' and 'Rallies' as ways people show their needs
Write the slogan: 'Government is accountable to the people'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the basic loop of democratic accountability
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of the diagram showing the people-government link (10 marks)
